coredata.dc:title: Biogenic carbon dot-based fluorescence-mediated immunosensor for the detection of disease biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Deb, Ankita
Nalkar, Gaurav Raghunath
Chowdhury, Devasish
coredata.dc:description: The dynamic interplay of nanotechnology and immunoassay has unlocked an arena for developing next-generation techniques to contribute to detecting disease biomarkers. Herein, the work establishes the strategic fabrication of an immunosensor by sandwich technique based on the fluorescence phenomenon of carbon dots for the detection of the disease biomarker VEGF (Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor). VEGF, a biomarker for angiogenesis, is considered cancerous if found in elevated levels in the blood, and so is paramount for early detection of disease. Carbon dots derived from a biogenic source were synthesized employing a green microwave-assisted method followed by conjugating with a detection antibody, human immunoglobulin G (IgG), via EDC-NHS amidation reaction. On the other hand, the VEGF biomarker was immobilized onto the capture antibody. The detection antibody tagged with the fluorescent probe is employed as a bridge to connect with the VEGF biomarkers bound to the capture antibody. The response to different concentrations of VEGF biomarkers was recorded in terms of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. The fluorescence immunosensor could exhibit a wide linear range of 0.1 fg/mL to 10 pg/mL with a low detection limit of 5.65 pg/mL towards VEGF. The potentiality of this designed immunosensor was qualitatively assessed with human blood plasma samples, showing promising results, thereby upholding the applicability of carbon dots as fluorescent labels in immunoassay techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Antibody
Biomarker
Immunosensor
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of OCD with IgG antibody
Fluorescence immunoassay for the determination of VEGF
VEGF immunoassay in anticoagulant human blood plasma

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank IASST for the in-house project. The authors also thank SAIC, IASST for instrumentation facilities. AD thanks Dr. Tulsi K. Joishy for helping with blood experiments. AD thanks IASST for the fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurement, (b) representative TEM image, (c) corresponding particle size distribution histogram, and (d) PL emission spectra of OCD.
(a) Stacked FT-IR spectra, (b) UV–vis spectra, and (c) PL emission spectra of OCD and IgG-OCD at λ ext 390 nm (inset: photographs of OCD and IgG-OCD under UV light).

(a) Stacked PL emission spectra of IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) ensemble at various concentrations of VEGF biomarker at λ ext 390 nm, (b) Plot of F/F0 vs. logarithmic concentration of VEGF. Inset (c): Linear region plot of F/F0 vs. log [VEGF].
Histogram showing the selectivity of the fluorescent probe, IgG-OCD, towards IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) entity in the presence of different coexisting substances.
(a) Stacked PL emission spectra of IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) entity at various concentrations of VEGF biomarker in blood plasma, and (b) comparative PL emission spectra of OCD, IgG-OCD, and RS-1.
Pictorial representation of conjugation experiment between OCDs with IgG via carbodiimide chemistry.
Analytical performance data of the immunosensor towards detection of VEGF.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *SR is the standard error of regression.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparative data of different sensors designed for the detection of VEGF.

row.entry: Au star@MGITC@SiO2 on Au triangle nanoarray

head.ce:title: Biogenic carbon dot-based fluorescence-mediated immunosensor for the detection of disease biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati, 781035, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati, 781035, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Guwahati, 781035, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dynamic interplay of nanotechnology and immunoassay has unlocked an arena for developing next-generation techniques to contribute to detecting disease biomarkers. Herein, the work establishes the strategic fabrication of an immunosensor by sandwich technique based on the fluorescence phenomenon of carbon dots for the detection of the disease biomarker VEGF (Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor). VEGF, a biomarker for angiogenesis, is considered cancerous if found in elevated levels in the blood, and so is paramount for early detection of disease. Carbon dots derived from a biogenic source were synthesized employing a green microwave-assisted method followed by conjugating with a detection antibody, human immunoglobulin G (IgG), via EDC-NHS amidation reaction. On the other hand, the VEGF biomarker was immobilized onto the capture antibody. The detection antibody tagged with the fluorescent probe is employed as a bridge to connect with the VEGF biomarkers bound to the capture antibody. The response to different concentrations of VEGF biomarkers was recorded in terms of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. The fluorescence immunosensor could exhibit a wide linear range of 0.1 fg/mL to 10 pg/mL with a low detection limit of 5.65 pg/mL towards VEGF. The potentiality of this designed immunosensor was qualitatively assessed with human blood plasma samples, showing promising results, thereby upholding the applicability of carbon dots as fluorescent labels in immunoassay techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Engulfed by the complex biological environment, interpreting the physiological condition of a human body is a promising task. The presence of a wide range of biological molecules, electrolytes, minerals, and dissolved gases encompassing body fluids, viz. blood, saliva, urine, and lymph, serve as a catalog in determining the health status of an individual as their amounts said to vary under physiological and pathological conditions [1]. Among the biological molecules, biomarkers refer to specific molecules released from cells or organs that indicate the presence of a disease in the body. From their characteristics, assessments are conducted to interpret whether the response is physiological, pathological, or pharmacological to a therapeutic intervention [2]. However, these circulating biomarkers present in picomolar or even lower concentrations in blood are reported to be associated with early-stage cancers and other infectious diseases [3]. Therefore, detecting biomarkers prevailing at relatively low abundance, even much lower than plasma proteins [4], in the complex biological environment is formidable. Early on, the detection approach started with traditional techniques such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), Western blot, radioimmunoassay, and mass spectrometry [5–8]. The complexity of these methods' requirement for expertise to carry out these assays and analyses has limited their use in detection methods. Besides, the performance of the detection tool, that is, the biosensor, demands a low limit of detection (LOD) and a point-of-care (POC) approach for rapid, high-throughput diagnosis and analysis at an affordable rate. Based on these requirements, bioanalytical processes were undertaken to design biosensors based on electrochemical, surface plasmon resonance (SPR), or fluorescent phenomenon [9–11], where all these methods have experienced unprecedented growth within a short period.

Among all the approaches, fluorescent-mediated biomarker detection has created a niche owing to its simplicity in designing, functioning, and instrumentation technique. They offer several advantages-availability of fluorescent probes in a broad spectral region, customization of the probe to act in a particular spectral range, non-destructive operational route, and rapid signal generation [11]. The fabrication strategy is based on antigen-antibody binding and tagging of the fluorescent probe [12]. Such technique is referred to as fluorescence immunoassay, where the assay categorically differs from each other in terms of the arrangement of the antigen-antibody immunocomplex as well as placement of the fluorescent probe in the immunocomplex [11]. The unique ability of this method to recognize and bind specifically to target molecules has gained prominence in biomedical research and clinical diagnosis [13,14]. The use of nanoparticles (NPs), like semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), metal NPs and silica NPs as fluorescent probes in biomarker detection has been documented. For example, Zhang and his co-workers reported redox-mediated indirect fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of α-fetoprotein (AFP), a cancer biomarker using dopamine (DA)-functionalized CdSe/ZnS QDs [15]. The concentration of AFP was understood by the fluorescence intensity signal of QDs connected via tyrosinase conjugated detection antibody. The system obtained a LOD of 10 pM for AFP detection. Another work reported that dual detection of platelet-derived growth factor-two B subunits (PDGF-BB) and thrombin was achieved by aptamer-modified silver NPs immobilized on streptavidin microarrays forming a sandwich structure [16]. The system obtained a LOD of 625 pM for PDGF-BB and 21 pM for thrombin. However, these NPs suffer from certain drawbacks like aqueous solubility, quick aggregation, delivery difficulty, and toxicity in live cells [17].

On the other hand, carbon nanomaterials have emerged as a potential candidate compared to other NPs discussed above. Carbon nanomaterials, specifically carbon dots (CDs) defined as zero-dimensional nanomaterials, have additional features of high aqueous solubility, easy synthetic route of preparation, high resistivity towards photobleaching, non-toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [18]. Therefore, the applicability of CDs has been increasingly in demand in biosensing applications [19–21]. However, the exploration of CDs as a fluorescent probe in antigen-antibody studies of biomarker detection is developing. Only a few works have been reported so far. Zhu et al. synthesized CDs from citric acid, post-functionalized them to form carboxylated CDs, labeled them with an antibody, and used them for detecting human immunoglobulin G via creating antigen-antibody immunocomplex [22]. Following a similar immunocomplex formation, Othman et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs from the same source to detect NMP22 biomarkers by labelling the CDs with monoclonal antibodies [23].

Herein, the current study exemplifies using CDs as fluorescent labels via the construction of a sandwich-type fluorescence immunosensor. Such configuration requires a capture antibody (cAB), the antigen/analyte of interest, and a detection antibody (dAB) conjugated to a fluorophore to build cAB^antigen^dAB immunocomplex [11]. Sandwich-type assays are known for the high specificity and selectivity an antibody has for an analyte [24]. In our work, IgG antibody is used as both cAB and dAB to bind to the antigen, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is selected as the antigen/target analyte. VEGF is a protein biomarker for angiogenesis that promotes the migration and proliferation of endothelial cells [25]. It is also related to tumour-associated angiogenesis [25] and is upregulated in several forms of cancer. Targeting and detecting this antigen by administrating a therapeutic antibody to treat selected malignancies is crucial [26,27]. The key point of this study emphasizes the use of natural/biogenic precursor to synthesize CDs and its ability to couple directly to dAB to act as a fluorescent label. Coupling with antibodies requires specific functional groups. Unlike other studies, post-functionalization and heteroatom doping in CDs were carried out to enable coupling with the antibodies [22,23]. In this work, the parameters set during the synthesis were optimized so that the CDs possessed the necessary functionalities to enable direct protein labelling. Here, the dAB acts as a bridge to pass the fluorescence signal of CDs to the antigen bound on cAB, confirmed by the enhancement of fluorescence intensity with the increased biomarker concentration. The working mechanism of the designed immunosensor for biomarker detection was also qualitatively assessed with human blood plasma samples showing desirable results. In a nutshell, this work demonstrates the applicability and potentiality of CDs in fabricating immunosensor, thereby creating avenues for clinical diagnosis.

N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), Immunoglobulin G (IgG) from human serum, and recombinant human VEGF165 were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The purity of IgG is greater than 95% by SDS electrophoresis, whereas for VEGF165, the purity is greater than 98% by both SDS-PAGE and HPLC analyses. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were procured from Himedia. 10X phosphate-buffered saline (10X PBS, pH 7.4) purchased from SRL was used in preparing these solutions. Other chemicals such as citric acid anhydrous, glycine, Na2HPO4, and KH2PO4 were purchased from SRL, whereas, CaCl2 fused, MgCl2 hexahydrate, and d(+)-Glucose, anhydrous was provided by Merck. Sweet oranges (Citrus sinensis) were collected from the local fruit market of Boragaon, Guwahati, India. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments. Blood sample was obtained from a healthy individual following written informed consent according to the Ethics Committee for Human Studies of the Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology.

Monowave 200, a microwave synthesis reactor of Anton Paar, was used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from sweet orange juice. Particle size and zeta potential were determined by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) using Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries Nano ZS90. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was taken to draw insights into the size and morphology of CDs (model: JEOL-2100 plus, at 200 kV operating voltage). Absorption and emission spectra were recorded in Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer UV-2600 and Jasco Spectrofluorometer FP-8300, respectively. IR was taken in the PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer instrument by making KBr pellets and scanned within the range of 500–4000 cm−1 to understand the structural changes that occurred during the reactions. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on Picosecond Time-Resolved Fluorimeter (LifeSpec II, Edinburg Instruments) with a 375 nm pulse laser excitation source. The decay profiles were fitted by the multi-exponential function provided by the F980 software to calculate the lifetime of the systems.

The protocol involves a microwave-assisted pathway in preparing the carbon dots. 7.5 mL of freshly prepared sweet orange juice dispersed in 2.5 mL of distilled water was put in a G30 reaction vial and then subjected to microwave treatment for 5 min at a temperature of 160 °C. The pressure and power of the microwave reactor were in the range of 10–12 bar and 20–23 W, respectively, throughout the synthesis. Soon after the treatment, the subsequent steps involve purifying the solution via centrifuging at 7500 rpm for 30 min to remove large and unreacted particles, followed by filtering the supernatant with a 0.22 μm syringe filter. The resulting solution was stored in a refrigerator at 8 °C until further use. The synthesized sweet orange carbon dots are designated as OCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of OCD with IgG antibody
ce:section.ce:para: Carbodiimide coupling reaction was carried out to conjugate OCD with IgG, which involves the use of EDC/NHS reagents. The method goes as follows: to 1 mL OCD, 100 μL of each of EDC (100 mM) and NHS (25 mM) are added consecutively, each being subjected to incubation at 37 °C for 30 min at 100 rpm. Then, to the solution, 50 μL of 1 mg/mL IgG antibody solution is added and again incubated in the same environment for 2 h. The conjugate solution was centrifuged to discard any unreacted particles if any, and finally kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C until further use in analysing and carrying out other assays. The solution is termed IgG-OCD. It is to be noted that the IgG tagged to OCD is regarded as the detection antibody.

Quantum yield of both the system, OCD, and IgG-OCD were calculated by comparing absorbances of different concentrations of both the systems with integrated fluorescence intensities taking quinine sulfate as reference (QY = 0.54 at 360 nm, in 0.1 M H2SO4), using the following equation-
φ =
φ R
×
(
I /
I R
)
×
(
A R
/ A
)
×
(
η 2
/
η R 2
)
where, φ is the quantum yield (QY), I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance, η is the refractive index. The subscript R denotes the reference chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence immunoassay for the determination of VEGF
ce:section.ce:para: First, 100 μL of capture antibody (IgG, 1 mg/mL, prepared in 10X PBS, pH 7.4) was added to reaction vials and incubated overnight at 4 °C. Different concentrations of VEGF ranging from 100 ng/mL to 0.1 fg/mL were prepared by serial dilutions, from which 100 μL of every VEGF solution was subsequently added to the reaction vials containing capture antibody and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. In the final step, 100 μL of the prepared IgG-OCD conjugate was added to all the solutions of different VEGF concentrations and incubated under the same environment for 30 min to generate IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) sandwich immunocomplex. The resulting immunocomplex is then subjected to fluorescence measurement. The fluorescence intensities at a particular excitation wavelength are recorded thrice that are obtained by the immunoassay experiments done in triplicate to represent the error bars and standard deviations of the measurements.

The pre-treatment of human blood samples was carried out to extract the blood plasma. In the process, human blood sample collected from a healthy individual is injected into anticoagulated vials. The anticoagulated blood is centrifuged in 3000 rcf for 15 min at 4 °C to obtain cell-free plasma and stored at −20 °C until analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: VEGF immunoassay in anticoagulant human blood plasma
ce:section.ce:para: First, the human blood plasma was spiked with known concentrations of VEGF solution, such as 0.25 ng/mL, 0.50 ng/mL, 0.75 ng/mL, and 1 ng/mL. Then the fluorescence immunoassay of the VEGF-spiked blood plasma samples was carried out by following the same protocol, as explained in Section 2.3.4., except for replacing VEGF stock solutions with that of the spiked blood plasma samples.

The presence of appropriate functionalities in biogenic sources contributes to synthesizing carbon dots with desired functionalities, thereby aiding in designing fluorescent probes for biosensing applications. At the outset of this work, sweet orange juice underwent microwave treatment to generate a yellowish-brown solution illuminated with blue fluorescence when perceived under UV light of wavelength 365 nm. Thus, the formation of carbon dots derived from sweet orange juice (OCDs) was preliminarily understood. Further confirmation of dot formation was verified from size distribution profiles obtained via two characterization techniques: Zeta-seizer and TEM. The average size of OCDs showed to be about 3 nm (Fig. 1 a) in DLS measurement. Fig. 1b represents the TEM image of OCD portraying the particles to be well-dispersed and representing a narrow size distribution of 3–5.5 nm, obtained from the particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 1c). The two results being tallied with each other corroborate the formation of OCDs below 10 nm. The presence of glucose, fructose, citric acid, and ascorbic acid acted as carbon bio-precursors in simultaneously contributing towards carbonization in creating the carbon dot. Given the underlying fluorescent nature of OCDs, emission spectra are recorded at a series of excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 1d). The entire emission window depicts a gradual increment of the emission peak, reaching maximum, then decreasing with increasing excitation wavelength. Another behavioural characteristic observed for OCDs is excitation-dependent emission; the PL emission peak shifts towards longer wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelengths. Such spectra indicate variable emissive sites generated in the carbon dot.

The next step undertaken was that of conjugating the synthesized OCDs with IgG via carbodiimide coupling reaction. This reaction is mainly known for covalently conjugating antibodies with the surface functional groups of nanoparticles [22,28]. The carbodiimide chemistry is based on the activation of carboxyl groups by EDC and NHS for direct reaction with amino groups (-NH2) present in IgG to generate amides. Nevertheless, the rationale behind the choice is the presence of –COOH groups on the surface of OCDs. Scheme 1 portrays the conjugation of OCD and IgG via forming –CONH– linkages between them. The presence of –COOH functional groups of OCD can be speculated from the characteristic broadened band around 3357 cm−1 and another band at 1723 cm−1, referring to O–H and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, in the FT-IR spectrum of OCD (Fig. 2 a). Other bands located at 1621 cm−1 correspond to CC stretch, 1411 cm−1 to O–H bend, and 1068 cm−1 to C–O stretch. The presence of these polar moieties has led to good dispersibility of OCDs in the aqueous medium. To validate the conjugation of IgG with OCDs via amidation reaction, the FT-IR spectrum of IgG-OCD (Fig. 2a) provides evidence of the amide formation. The appearance of CO stretch at a lower vibrational frequency of 1641 cm−1 compared to that of OCDs gives evidence of the characteristic appearance of this carbonyl functionality owing to the formation of the amide group. The zeta potentials were also recorded to know the surface charge involved for both OCDs and IgG-OCD. The zeta potential value of OCDs is −15.2 mV, whereas, for OCD-IgG, the value is −6.93 mV. Despite the negative charge of both systems, the lesser magnitude of negative zeta potential of IgG-OCD might be reasoned by the accumulation or conjugation of IgG antibodies onto the carbon dot system [29]. A UV–visible measurement was also performed for both systems (Fig. 2b), wherein both OCD and IgG-OCD have their absorbance around 283 nm, which corresponds to n→π* transition exhibiting CO functional moiety. A notable feature supporting the formation of amide linkage in IgG-OCD is the absorbance at 210 nm, which directly corresponds to the n→π* transition arising exclusively due to the amide chromophore unit [30]. Moreover, there is a hyperchromic effect in the whole spectra window of IgG-OCD, indicating the labelling of IgG antibodies onto the OCD system [22]. We have also recorded the fluorescence spectra of the conjugated system at a chosen excitation wavelength to examine changes that occurred, if any, compared to the carbon dot system. It could be seen from the PL spectra (Fig. 2c) that there is a two-fold increase in fluorescence intensity of the antibody-conjugated carbon dot system, IgG-OCD, over OCD. To understand the increment of the system's intensity after conjugation, quantum yield (QY) and average lifetimes of OCD and IgG-OCD were calculated. The results were quite intriguing. The QY value was nearly similar, 17.8% for OCD and 17.25% for IgG-OCD, whereas the average lifetimes (τav) values were calculated to be 2 ns for OCD and 1.75 ns for IgG-OCD. The average lifetime is calculated from the individual lifetime components obtained from the multi-exponential decay fit profiles, as discussed in the Supplementary Information. Accordingly, the radiative rate constant (kr) was also calculated using the formula kr = QY/τav, and the values obtained for OCD and IgG-OCD were 0.08 ns−1 and 0.098 ns−1, respectively. Thus, the increase in fluorescence intensity after conjugation can be comprehended by the increase of kr value, subsequently holding up the fact that fluorescence lifetime is independent of fluorescence intensity [31]. Therefore, all the above characterizations are in corroboration of the successful conjugation or labelling of the antibody IgG to OCD. Note that the antibody tagged with the OCDs is the detection antibody (dAB).

After the subsequent demonstration of IgG antibody labelling with the fluorescent probe, OCD, we utilized this conjugate as a bridge to attach with the target analyte/antigen, VEGF biomarker, in a specific way. Scheme 2 instantiates the overall arrangement of the ensemble. That is being executed to detect the target analyte. The procedure can be divided into two parts-first, the capture antibody (cAB) was incubated with the analyte solution of different concentrations. During incubation, the VEGF biomarker (analyte) gets bound to the cAB (as represented by the ‘cAB-biomarker ensemble’ in Scheme 2). Second, this cAB-biomarker ensemble was again incubated with the detection antibody (dAB) conjugated with the fluorescent OCD, IgG-OCD system. Therefore, the architectural design of the ensemble can be viewed as an antibody-antigen-antibody association that is precisely termed sandwich configuration. Besides, this type of design is supported based on the antigen, possessing at least two binding sites that could bind with both dAB and cAB [32], making the sandwich assays more sensitive and specific [11,33]. The entire entity, IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD), was then subjected to fluorescence measurement. The response is generated based on the different biomarker concentrations in the prepared entities, which could be detected via the difference in fluorescence intensity signals of the detectable unit (IgG-OCD) of the IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) entity. In our case, IgG was used as both cAB and dAB owing to its lesser cost than that of corresponding VEGF antibodies, thereby simultaneously simplifying the system's overall design.

Fig. 3 a displays the fluorescence signals of IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD), recorded at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm at different VEGF concentrations ranging from nanomolar to femtomolar levels, such as 1 ng/mL, 100 pg/mL, 10 pg/mL, 1 pg/mL, 10 fg/mL and 0.1 fg/mL. Also, a blank has been prepared by adding buffer only to the entity, replacing the VEGF solution, and accordingly, the fluorescence signal is recorded. From the spectra, it could be anticipated that fluorescent intensity has increased with the increase of biomarker concentration. To have a clearer picture, Fig. 3b describes the course of relative fluorescent intensity changes with respect to various VEGF concentrations in the entity via plotting (F/F0) vs. log [VEGF]. Here, F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) at different VEGF concentrations and blank, respectively. The plot depicted an increment in the relative fluorescence intensity accompanied by increased biomarker concentration, reaching a maximum at 10 pg/mL. The mechanism behind this increasing fluorescence intensity can be explained as follows: the photoluminescence of a fluorescent substance depends on the feasibility of the electron-hole recombination process, which relies on surface defects and traps generated. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity of the antigen-antibody induced immunocomplex might be due to the surface passivation on the OCDs, creating extrinsic surface states and thereby leading to radiative recombination of electron-holes at the surface vacancies and simultaneously reducing the trap states of the OCDs [22,34].

With the further increased biomarker concentration, fluorescence saturation occurred, as seen in Fig. 3b. Such observation was also reported by Sun et al. [35]. The curve in the inset (Fig. 3c) describes a good linear relationship obtained between VEGF and the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) in the range of 0.1 fg/mL-10 pg/mL. The immunosensor achieved a lower detection limit (LOD) for VEGF to be 5.65 pg/mL. Table 1 showcases the overall analytical performance of our designed immunosensor, depicting the linear detection range, regression equation, correlation coefficient, and LOD, evaluated from Fig. 3c. Furthermore, it could be emphasized that the concentration of VEGF in the blood plasma of healthy individuals is reported in the range of 0–115 pg/mL [36]. Therefore, owing to the capability of the present system to have a wide linear range and LOD at such concentration, this fluorescence-mediated detection exhibited good sensitivity and, therefore, would be beneficial for the sensitive detection of disease biomarkers in clinical applications. Table 2 summarizes the performance of different sensing systems in terms of linear range and LOD specifying the method used for detection, as documented in the literature, and comparing it to the present sensor system. It could be observed that the current system could achieve a good LOD via fluorescence-mediated detection.

Apart from sensitivity, the selectivity of the sensor system is also a decisive factor to consider in assessing the sensor's potentiality. Henceforth, the selectivity of the system was checked via the response of the fluorescent probe/detectable moiety, IgG-OCD, towards the IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) entity by recording the fluorescence intensity of the entity in the presence of various kinds of probable coexisting substances such as BSA, citric acid, glucose, glycine, CaCl2, MgCl2, Na2HPO4, and KH2PO4. These coexisting substances are commonly found in blood plasma and are referred to as interfering agents (IAs). The interference study was carried out with the entity having 1 pg/mL VEGF solution, keeping the concentrations of all the coexisting substances at 1 μg/mL. Fig. 4 represents the histogram displaying the change in relative fluorescence intensity in terms of (F–F0)/F0 with the IAs. Here, F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) in the presence of IAs and the detectable moiety, IgG-OCD, respectively.

The interference of the mentioned coexisting substances is comprehended by calculating the %relative error, wherein the occurrence of value <±5% of the substances is not considered interfering [22]. Table S1 in Supplementary Information sums up the %relative error of all corresponding data extracted from the histogram. It could be elucidated that despite choosing a relatively higher concentration of the substances in the interference study, they did not result in any significant fluorescent change in comparison to the entity as they were within the tolerance limit mentioned, except that of glucose, whose relative error of 5.5% exhibited a slight interference. Thus, the interference study synopsizes good selectivity of the IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD) entity over different substances.

Fostered by the good sensitivity and selectivity of the designed sandwich entity towards detecting VEGF, the efficiency of the system was further appraised qualitatively with real samples, that is, blood plasma. However, in this aspect, due to the unavailability of diseased patient samples, the real samples were prepared by spiking optimized VEGF amounts into citrated blood plasma samples such that they resemble diseased samples. A total of five plasma samples were prepared, named RS-1, RS-2, RS-3, RS-4, and RS-5. RS-1 represents the citrated blood plasma, and RS-2, RS-3, RS-4, and RS-5 represent the citrated blood plasma spiked with 0.25 ng/mL, 0.50 ng/mL, 0.75 ng/mL, 1 ng/mL of VEGF solution, respectively.

Ascribing the fact that the normal range of VEGF in the blood plasma of a healthy individual lies in picomolar concentration and that the increase to nanomolar range indicates the growth of diseased or cancerous cells [32,36], henceforth, the spiking of the said concentrations in blood plasma would be wise enough to reflect as that of diseased cells. The PL emission spectra were recorded for all five real samples (RS) at λ ext 390 nm to check the response towards various VEGF biomarker concentrations in blood plasma in the designed sandwich entity, IgG^VEGF^(IgG-OCD). Fig. 5 a shows the stacked emission spectra of the entity, in which the plot portrays the increase of fluorescence intensity with increased spiked VEGF biomarker concentrations in blood plasma, a characteristic observed for sandwich assay [11]. The result also tallies the entity's response with the pure analyte/antigen, VEGF (Fig. 3a). Another comparison in terms of fluorescence intensity was made to validate further the response of the real samples observed in Fig. 5a. The stacked PL emission spectra (Fig. 5b) show an enhancement of fluorescence intensity of RS-1, indicating the underlying efficiency of the system in real samples.

In summary, this work demonstrates the fabrication of a sandwich-type immunosensor mediated by fluorescence phenomenon. CDs from sweet orange were obtained with good quantum yield and then subjected to directly conjugate with IgG antibody generating a detectable fluorescent probe, IgG-OCD. A fluorescence immunoassay technique was constructed with this IgG-OCD moiety by acting as a medium to pass the fluorescent signal to the analyte, VEGF biomarker bound to the capture antibody, IgG. The system responded to the different biomarker concentrations, exhibiting a good linear range of 0.1 fg/mL-10 pg/mL with LOD as low as 5.65 pg/mL, highlighting the sensitivity of the immunosensor. The system also showed satisfactory selectivity with probable interfering substances. Finally, the immunosensor could detect the presence of VEGF biomarker with spiked blood plasma samples. Although the work demonstrated here is a proof of the concept and will need more trials before it can be used in diagnostic set-up but current study could demonstrate successfully the use of naturally-derived carbon dots to design biosensors via immunoassay techniques, further boosting its applicability in biomedical research.
Supplementary Information: The file includes the fluorescence lifetime values, equation of average lifetime, and table showing % relative error.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank IASST for the in-house project. The authors also thank SAIC, IASST for instrumentation facilities. AD thanks Dr. Tulsi K. Joishy for helping with blood experiments. AD thanks IASST for the fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.340808.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proteomic technologies for the identification of disease biomarkers in serum: advances and challenges ahead

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ray, P.J. Reddy, R. Jain, K. Gollapalli, A. Moiyadi, S. Srivastava, Proteomic technologies for the identification of disease biomarkers in serum: advances and challenges ahead, Proteomics 11 (2011) 2139-2161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomarkers and surrogate endpoints: preferred definitions and conceptual framework

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.B.D. Working, Biomarkers and surrogate endpoints: preferred definitions and conceptual framework, Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 69 (2001) 89-95.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-molecule enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay detects serum proteins at subfemtomolar concentrations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Rissin, C.W. Kan, T.G. Campbell, S.C. Howes, D.R. Fournier, L. Song, D.C. Duffy, Single-molecule enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay detects serum proteins at subfemtomolar concentrations, Nat. Biotechnol. 28 (2010) 595-599.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microarray methods for protein biomarker detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.J. Lee, A.W. Wark, R.M. Corn, Microarray methods for protein biomarker detection, Analyst 133 (2008) 975-983.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immunosensors for detection of Annexin II and MUC5AC for early diagnosis of lung cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Kim, H.B. Noh, D.S. Park, S.H. Ryu, J.S. Koo, Y.B. Shim, Immunosensors for detection of Annexin II and MUC5AC for early diagnosis of lung cancer, Biosens. Bioelectron. 25 (2009) 456-462.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced gold nanoparticle-based ELISA for a breast cancer biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ambrosi, F. Airo, A. Merkoci, Enhanced gold nanoparticle-based ELISA for a breast cancer biomarker, Anal. Chem. 82 (2010) 1151-1156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiplexed immunoassay: quantitation and profiling of serum biomarkers using magnetic nanoprobes and MALDI-TOF MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Y. Wang, S.A. Chuang, P.C. Lin, L.S. Huang, S.H. Chen, S. Ouarda, Y.J. Chen, Multiplexed immunoassay: quantitation and profiling of serum biomarkers using magnetic nanoprobes and MALDI-TOF MS, Anal. Chem. 80 (2008) 6159-6167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A status report on field-portable immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Van Emon, C.L. Gerlach, A status report on field-portable immunoassay, Environ. Sci. Technol. 29 (1995) 312A-317A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical immunosensors for detection of cancer protein biomarkers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.V. Chikkaveeraiah, A.A. Bhirde, N.Y. Morgan, H.S. Eden, X. Chen, Electrochemical immunosensors for detection of cancer protein biomarkers, ACS nano 6 (2012) 6546-6561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface plasmon resonance based immunosensor for the detection of the cancer biomarker carcinoembryonic antigen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Altintas, Y. Uludag, Y. Gurbuz, I.E. Tothill, Surface Plasmon Resonance Based Immunosensor for the Detection of the Cancer Biomarker Carcinoembryonic Antigen, Talanta 86 (2011) 377-383.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence sensing of circulating diagnostic biomarkers using molecular probes and nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Tagit, N. Hildebrandt, Fluorescence sensing of circulating diagnostic biomarkers using molecular probes and nanoparticles, ACS Sens. 2 (2017) 31-45.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Distance-independent quenching of quantum dots by nanoscale-graphene in self-assembled sandwich immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liu, H. Zhao, X. Quan, S. Chen, X. Fan, Distance-independent quenching of quantum dots by nanoscale-graphene in self-assembled sandwich immunoassay, ChemComm. 46 (2010) 7909-7911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unbiased binding assays for discovering small-molecule probes and drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Kemp, M. Weiwer, A.N. Koehler, Unbiased binding assays for discovering small-molecule probes and drugs, Bioorg. Med. Chem. 20 (2012) 1979−1989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent immunosensor based on CuS nanoparticles for sensitive detection of cancer biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.D. Zhu, J. Peng, L.P. Jiang, J. J. Zhu, Fluorescent immunosensor based on CuS nanoparticles for sensitive detection of cancer biomarker, Analyst 139 (2014) 649-655.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Redox-mediated indirect fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of disease biomarkers using dopamine-functionalized quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.H. Zhang, W. Ma, Y.T. Long, Redox-mediated indirect fluorescence immunoassay for the detection of disease biomarkers using dopamine-functionalized quantum dots, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 5131-5136.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, H. Li, D. Xu, Aptamers-based sandwich assay for silver-enhanced fluorescence multiplex detection, Anal. Chim. Acta 905 (2016) 149−155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots versus organic dyes as fluorescent labels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Resch-Genger, M. Grabolle, S. Cavaliere-Jaricot, R. Nitschke, T. Nann, Quantum dots versus organic dyes as fluorescent labels, Nat. Methods 5 (2008) 763-775.
S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 49 (2010) 6726-6744.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, J. Li, Y. Jiang, S. Yang, W. Tan, R. Yang, Combination of π-π stacking and electrostatic repulsion between carboxylic carbon nanoparticles and fluorescent oligonucleotides for rapid and sensitive detection of thrombin, ChemComm. 47 (2011) 11321-11323.

Carbon Dots: Next-Generation Materials for Biomedical Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhu, X. Cui, J. Wu, Z. Wang, P. Wang, Y. Hou, M. Yang, Fluorescence immunoassay based on carbon dots as labels for the detection of human immunoglobulin G, Anal. Methods 6 (2014) 4430-4436.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. O. Othman, F. Salehnia, M. Hosseini, R. Hassan, A. Faizullah, M. R. Ganjali, Fluorescence immunoassay based on nitrogen doped carbon dots for the detection of human nuclear matrix protein NMP22 as biomarker for early-stage diagnosis of bladder cancer, Microchem. J. 157 (2020) 104966.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.B. Chinen, C.M. Guan, J.R. Ferrer, S.N. Barnaby, T.J. Merkel, C.A. Mirkin, Nanoparticle probes for the detection of cancer biomarkers, cells, and tissues by fluorescence, Chem. Rev. 115 (2015) 10530-10574.
N. Ferrara, R.S. Kerbel, Angiogenesis as a therapeutic target, Nature 438 (2005) 967-974.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Ferrara, Vascular endothelial growth factor: basic science and clinical progress, Endocr. Rev. 25 (2004) 581-611.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Storkebaum, D. Lambrechts, P. Carmeliet, VEGF: once regarded as a specific angiogenic factor, now implicated in neuroprotection, Bioassays 26 (2004) 943-954.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.Y. Berezin, S. Achilefu, Fluorescence lifetime measurements and biological imaging, Chem. Rev. 110 (2010) 2641-2684.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three-dimensional hierarchical plasmonic nano-architecture enhanced surface-enhanced Raman scattering immunosensor for cancer biomarker detection in blood plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, S.K. Cushing, J. Zhang, S. Suri, R. Evans, W.P. Petros, N. Wu, Three-dimensional hierarchical plasmonic nano-architecture enhanced surface-enhanced Raman scattering immunosensor for cancer biomarker detection in blood plasma, ACS nano 7 (2013) 4967-4976.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jaras, B. Adler, A. Tojo, J. Malm, G. Marko-Varga, H. Lilja, T. Laurell, Porous silicon antibody microarrays for quantitative analysis: Measurement of free and total PSA in clinical plasma samples, Clin. Chim. Acta 414 (2012) 76−84.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, W. Yang, R. Y. Lai, A folding-based electrochemical aptasensor for detection of vascular endothelial growth factor in human whole blood, Biosens. Bioelectron. 26 (2011) 2442-2447.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Malhotra, V. Patel, B.V. Chikkaveeraiah, B.S. Munge, S.C. Cheong, R.B. Zain, J.F. Rusling, (2012). Ultrasensitive detection of cancer biomarkers in the clinic by use of a nanostructured microfluidic array, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 6249-6255.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Pimkova, M. Bockova, K. Hegnerova, J. Suttnar, J. Cermak, J. Homola, J.E. Dyr, Surface Plasmon Resonance Biosensor for the Detection of VEGFR-1-a Protein Marker of Myelodysplastic Syndromes, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 402 (2012) 381 -387.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a more sensitive fluorescence sensing material for the detection of vascular endothelial growth factor, a biomarker for angiogenesis, prepared by combining a fluorescent peptide and a nanopillar substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Suzuki, K. Yokoyama, Construction of a More Sensitive Fluorescence Sensing Material for the Detection of Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor, a Biomarker for Angiogenesis, Prepared by Combining a Fluorescent Peptide and a Nanopillar Substrate, Biosens. Bioelectron. 26 (2011) 3696 -3699.
